one_line_takeaway: In sampling-based individual DP, one user's real privacy is silently determined by everyone else's budget choices, and this interdependence is a gameable attack surface: a central trainer or a colluding coalition can inflate a target's membership-inference risk (62% of targets on Credit Card Default, privacy score up to +0.5) without ever violating the formal (epsilon_i, delta_i) guarantee.
key_finding: The theoretically predicted interdependence manifests empirically: across nearly all tested datasets the privacy score (MIA susceptibility) of BOTH privacy groups rises when a smaller group holds a larger privacy budget, with Jonckheere-Terpstra significance at p <= 0.001 in almost every case. Exploiting this, the Budget Manipulation Attack succeeds against 62% of targeted individuals on the Credit Card Default dataset, raising the per-sample privacy score by up to 0.5; samples unaffected are attributed to the privacy onion effect [8]. The theoretical adversarial advantage for an epsilon_1=8 group in the presence of an epsilon_2 group ranges roughly 0.15 to 0.4 depending on proportion and epsilon_2. The sensitivity-based iDP ablation shows a trend an order of magnitude smaller, causally attributing the excess risk to the sampling-based mechanism's shared-parameter interdependence rather than measurement artifacts.
core_contribution: The paper identifies and formalizes a previously overlooked vulnerability in sampling-based individual differential privacy (iDP): because the mechanism (Boenisch et al.'s 'Have it your way' sampling-based DP-SGD, ref [5]) fixes an expected batch size, individual sampling rates are coupled through shared mechanism parameters. As a result, a user's true privacy risk (their 'excess vulnerability') is a function of the ENTIRE dataset's privacy-budget distribution, not solely their own chosen (epsilon_i, delta_i). This breaks iDP's core promise of individual privacy control even though every per-sample guarantee is formally met. Building on this, the authors introduce two novel attacks that operate entirely within the formal DP guarantees: (1) the Budget Manipulation Attack (a central trainer strategically assigns per-sample budgets to inflate a target's risk) and (2) the Collusion Attack (a coalition of data holders coordinates only their own budgets to amplify a victim's risk with no central authority). Finally they propose (epsilon_i, delta_i, Delta)-iDP, a privacy contract using the Delta-divergence (approximate Blackwell dominance, from Kaissis et al. [26]) to give users a hard upper bound on excess vulnerability relative to a non-individualized baseline mechanism. NOTE: the LiRA MIA (Carlini et al. [7]), the Delta-divergence definition [26], and the sampling-based iDP mechanism [5] are cited prior work that the paper builds ON, not its own inventions; the paper's own contributions are the interdependence finding, the two attacks, and the (epsilon_i, delta_i, Delta)-iDP contract.
threat_model: Two distinct threat models, both operating strictly within the stipulated (epsilon_i, delta_i)-iDP guarantees (no DP violation, no privileged model access). (1) Budget Manipulation Attack: a central adversary (the model trainer/owner) with NO access to the model, its parameters, gradients, or outputs; the attack is data-only and executed entirely BEFORE training. The adversary selects a target x_j and may set every sample's budget epsilon_i within a contractual per-sample upper bound [0, epsilon_max_i], choosing budgets to maximize the target's expected vulnerability V(x_j, A(D)). Effective strategy: assign the target its maximal budget while assigning very low budgets to all non-target points. (2) Adversarial Collusion Attack: a coalition C of data holders, each controlling only their OWN budget epsilon_i (cannot alter the target's or non-colluders' budgets), coordinating to maximize the target's vulnerability. No central authority required; especially relevant in federated learning where colluding data silos (e.g., 4 of 5 silos = 80% of budgets) can target the fifth silo. Both attacks surface but do not directly extract information about the target; they make any subsequent attack (e.g., membership inference) easier, and stay invisible to anyone auditing only the target's (epsilon_j, delta) at the calibration point.
limitations: Authors acknowledge: (1) MIA has known limited auditing capability, especially scaled to LLMs [60], so manifestation in large-scale/diverse-modality settings needs further study; (2) practical execution of the collusion attack depends on attackers' ability to coordinate and to accept a utility trade-off, which is context/domain dependent; (3) due to the privacy onion effect [8] not all samples expend their full budget, so the attack does not affect every target (some show no change); (4) they deliberately operate in a low-delta regime to amplify the measurable signal (the effect also exists at larger delta but with smaller magnitude), which the reviewer notes makes the reported effect sizes an emphasized rather than typical-deployment regime; (5) the proposed (epsilon_i, delta_i, Delta)-iDP is a mitigation DIRECTION that bounds excess risk, not a comprehensive practical defense, which remains an open challenge. The full attack evaluation is limited to the more powerful Budget Manipulation Attack on one dataset (Credit Card Default) due to the 256,000-shadow-model cost; the collusion attack's effect is inferred to scale with coalition proportion rather than measured end-to-end.

datasets_used: Credit Card Default (ε1=4, ε2=20; also used for the budget-manipulation attack evaluation)
MNIST (ε1=4, ε2=16 not significant; and ε1=[4,1000]/16, ε2=50 significant)
Peripheral Blood (used in sensitivity-based iDP ablation, Table II)
quantitative_results: Budget Manipulation Attack succeeds against 62% of targeted individuals (Credit Card Default, Figure 6)
Per-sample privacy score increases by up to 0.5 under the attack
Theoretical adversarial (MIA) advantage ranges ~0.15 to 0.4 for an ε1=8 group in the presence of an ε2 group at δ=1e-12
Jonckheere-Terpstra significance p <= 0.001 for the monotonic privacy-score trend on almost all datasets (Table I); MNIST at ε1=4/ε2=16 was not significant
Table I effect sizes (ES) e.g. UCI-Isolet group1 30.6 / group2 70.9; OrganSMNIST 34.6 / 71.8; CIFAR-10 13.9 / 53.5; Credit Card Default 20.2 / 17.5
Sensitivity-based iDP ablation trend is smaller by an order of magnitude than sampling-based iDP (Table II)
Attack evaluation trained m×b×n = 1000×4×64 = 256,000 shadow models; n=64 for per-target LiRA, n=512 default for the distribution study
Theoretical example scenario: 50,000 samples, batch size 128, 5 epochs; a colluding fraction as small as ~20% significantly affects others' excess risk
baselines_compared: Sampling-based iDP (Boenisch et al. [5], 'Have it your way') — the mechanism under attack
Sensitivity-based iDP (per-sample clipping bounds, also from [5]) — ablation whose privacy profiles are independent of the budget distribution
Standard non-individualized (ε, δ)-DP baseline — reference for the proposed (εi, δi, Δ)-iDP Blackwell-dominance bound
